gems.title: Pearl Index and Life Table Analysis
gems.content:
**What is “Pearl index”?**
Pearl index is used for measuring the failure rate of a contraceptive. It is defined as the number of contraception failures per 100 woman years (HWY) of exposure. It is calculated by the formula:
Failure rate per HWY = (Total accidental pregnancies / Total months of exposure) × 1200
*   The numerator “total accidental pregnancies” includes all unwanted pregnancies irrespective of the outcome, i.e.,
    – Livebirth
    – Abortion
    – Stillbirth or
    – Still not terminated
*   The denominator “total months of exposure” is obtained by deduction of the following from the total period of review:
    – 10 months for each full-term pregnancy
    – 4 months for each abortion
*   “1200” is the number of months in 100 years.
*   A minimum of 600 months of exposure is necessary for obtaining a valid “failure rate” value.
**What is the drawback of Pearl index and how does “life table analysis” address it?**
The drawback of the Pearl index is that it assumes a constant failure rate over time. This assumption is incorrect due to two reasons:
1. The most fertile couples would get pregnant at the beginning of the study and thus no longer be counted in the denominator. Couples remaining later in the study would usually have lower fertility rate.
2. With most birth control methods the effectiveness increases with experience. The longer a couple is in the study, the better it gets at using the method. So the longer the study length, the lower the Pearl Index would be. Hence comparisons of Pearl Indexes from studies of different lengths cannot be accurate.
“Life table analysis” calculates cumulative failure rates over a specified timeframe. They present failure rate as number of pregnancies per 100 women years, standardized by yearly cut-off points (usually 1, 3 or 5 years), e.g., “life-table rates for long-acting hormonal methods were reported as 0–0.6 per 100 HWY at one year.”
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Centchroman Tablets
gems.content:
**Name the active ingredient and what is its pharmaceutical action?**
The active ingredient is “Ormeloxifene” also known as “Centchroman.” Centchroman is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM). It has some estrogenic effect (e.g., in bones) and some antiestrogenic effect (e.g., in uterus and breast).
**What is the mechanism of action of centchroman?**
*   Its antiestrogenic activity on the endometrium inhibits the fertilized ovum from implantation.
*   It hastens the transfer of ovum from the fallopian tubes to the endometrial cavity, before it is ready for implantation.
**Mention the recommended dosage schedule for contraception.**
For the first 3 months: For initiation of the Centchroman (Ormeloxifene), the first pill is to be taken on the first day of period (as indicated by the first day of bleeding) and the second pill 3 days later. This pattern of days is repeated through the first 3 months.
After the initial 3 months: From the fourth month, the pill is to be taken once a week (the same day of the week on which the first pill was taken) and should be continued on the weekly schedule regardless of the menstrual cycle.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Anemia Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan (AMB) 7X7X7 Strategy
gems.content:
**What is the AMB 7X7X7 strategy?**
The 7X7X7 strategy includes seven beneficiary groups, seven interventions, and seven institutional mechanisms.
Seven beneficiary groups include the following:
*   Low Birth Weight (LBW) babies (0–6 months) [New]
*   Under-5 children / Preschool children (6–59 months)
*   Children aged 5–10 years
*   Adolescents (10–19 years)
*   Women of Reproductive Age (WRA) (15–49 years)
*   Pregnant women
*   Lactating mothers (0–6 months)
Seven interventions for control of anemia include the following:
*   Prophylactic IFA supplementation
*   Deworming (bi-annual)
*   Intensified Hemoglobin Testing (T4 strategy: Test, Treat, Talk, Track)
*   Treatment of anemia (including advanced clinical management like Intravenous Iron Therapy [Ferric Carboxymaltose] for severe cases)
*   Information and Communication campaign (BCC/Talk) focusing on behaviors like delayed cord clamping and dietary counseling
*   Mandatory provision of IFA-fortified food in all government-funded health programs
*   "Eating Right" approach: Promotion of daily consumption of diversified, iron-rich locally available foods, and addressing non-nutritional causes of anemia in endemic pockets (malaria, hemoglobinopathies, and fluorosis) [New]
Seven institutional mechanisms are set up to ensure accountability. These include the following:
*   Intra-ministerial coordination (National Steering Committee)
*   National Anemia Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan Unit: to assist and oversee the implementation of the strategy in states
*   National Center of Excellence and Advanced Research on Anemia Control (NCEAR-A): to keep improving the program by giving technical inputs
*   Convergence with other ministries via the ‘POSHAN abhiyaan’ platform
*   Strengthening supply chain and logistics
*   Unified Anemia Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan Portal (integrating data from JANANI, RBSK, and U-WIN portals) [New]
*   Digitally integrated Monitoring & Evaluation (M&E) framework for real-time tracking and accountability [New]
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023. Update: MoHFW, Government of India, Anemia Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan Guidelines, 2026.[/REF]

gems.title: Oral Rehydration Salt (ORS)
gems.content:
**What are the constituents?**
The composition of the new, reduced osmolarity ORS, as recommended by WHO, is as follows:
*   Sodium chloride—2.6 g
*   Glucose (anhydrous)—13.5 g
*   Potassium chloride—1.5 g
*   Trisodium citrate dehydrate—2.9 g
(Total weight of a packet = 20.5 g)
**What benefit does trisodium citrate offer over the use of sodium bicarbonate in ORS?**
Trisodium citrate has the following advantages over sodium bicarbonate:
*   It improves the shelf life of ORS.
*   It helps in reducing the stool output probably through a direct action on the intestinal mucosa, enhancing the absorption of sodium and water.
**How is the new ORS different from the older formulation?**
The new ORS contains lesser amount of sodium and glucose thereby resulting in a lower osmolarity solution after reconstitution.
The reduced osmolarity of the solution has been shown to have the following benefits in addition to correction of dehydration:
*   It reduces the stool output by 25% in comparison to the original formula.
*   It reduces vomiting by 30%.
*   It has been demonstrated to reduce the need for IV therapy by more than 30%.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Shakir Tape
gems.content:
**What are the cut-off values?**
For 6- to 59-month-old babies, the cut-off values are as follows:
*   Less than 11.5 cm (115 mm): Red zone—severe acute malnutrition
*   11.5–12.5 cm (125 mm): Yellow zone—moderate acute malnutrition
*   12.5 cm or more: Green zone—mild or NO malnutrition
**Explain the color zones seen on the tape.**
*   Red zone: Severe malnutrition
*   Yellow zone: Moderate malnutrition
*   Green zone: No malnutrition or mild malnutrition
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Vaccine Vial Monitor (VVM)
gems.content:
**What is vaccine vial monitor?**
Vaccine vial monitor (VVM) is a small label stuck on the vial of a vaccine which changes color as the vaccine vial is exposed to heat.
The color of the VVM central square indicates if the vaccine is safe for use or not. It is a small light purple circle with a white square in the center. The square remains white if there is no exposure to heat. With heat exposure the square begins to darken. It registers the cumulative heat exposure over time. The combined effects of time and temperature cause the inner square of the VVM to darken, gradually and irreversibly.
Till the time for which the center square is lighter than the surrounding circle the vaccine is safe for use. As soon as the square becomes the same color as the background or is darker, the heat may have damaged the vaccine and the vial should be discarded.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: The "Shake Test"
gems.content:
**What is “Shake test”?**
Shake test is used for determining if an adsorbed vaccine has been frozen anytime in the past. The test is valid for “adsorbed vaccines” only, such as DPT, DT, TT, Pentavalent vaccine, and Hepatitis-B.
**Steps of “Shake test”**
1. Take the suspected freeze damaged vial.
2. Take another vial of the same vaccine from the same batch and the same manufacturer and label it is as “control.”
3. Deliberately freeze the “control” and then thaw it at room temperature.
4. Shake both the vials vigorously.
5. Place both of them on a flat surface, side by side, against a source of light.
6. Observe and compare the sedimentation rate in both the bottles.
7. If the TEST vial sediments slower than the FROZEN vial then use the vaccine.
8. If the sedimentation is similar in both the vials OR the TEST vial sediments faster than the FROZEN vial then the vaccine is damaged, discard it.
(Tip to remember in Shake test: “Haste makes waste”—faster sedimentation indicates damage. Discard the vial.)
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Open Vial Policy (OVP)
gems.content:
**What is “open vial policy” (OVP)?**
Implementation of open vial policy (OVP) allows reuse of partially used multidose vials of applicable vaccines under the Universal Immunization Program (UIP) in subsequent sessions up to 4 weeks (28 days) subject to meeting certain conditions. This policy contributes to the reduction of vaccine wastage.
**OVP is applicable to which vaccines?**
Open vial policy is only applicable to
*   DPT
*   Td/TT
*   Hep B
*   OPV
*   Pentavalent vaccine
*   Injectable inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV)
*   PCV
**To which vaccines, the OVP is NOT applicable?**
Open Vial Policy does not apply to
*   Measles/MR
*   Rotavirus
*   BCG
*   JE vaccines
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Cold Chain Equipment & Holdover Times
gems.content:
**What is meant by the “holdover time” of cold chain equipment?**
In the event of power failure, “holdover time” for any cold chain equipment is defined as “the time taken by the equipment to raise the inside cabinet temperature from its cut-off temperature to the maximum temperature limit of its recommended range,” e.g., in the case of ILR, if the temperature is 4°C, then the time taken to reach 8°C from 4°C will be the holdover time for that ILR. 
**Holdover Time for Ice Lined Refrigerator (ILR):**
E.g., the holdover time for an ILR is minimum 20 hours at 43°C. 
**Holdover Time for Day Carrier:**
For how long can the day carrier maintain the temperature between +2°C and +8°C?
For 16–20 hours. 
**Holdover Time for Cold Box:**
For how long can the large cold box maintain the temperature between +2°C and +8°C?
Up to 5 days 
**Holdover Time for Deep Freezer:**
In case of power failure, the deep freezer can maintain the cabinet temperature in the range of –15°C to –25°C for 18 and 26 hours at ambient temperatures of 42°C and 32°C, respectively when full of frozen ice packs and if not opened. 
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Steps for identification of an adult mosquito
gems.content:
If an adult mosquito is kept for identification, it is most likely to be one of the three—Anopheles, Culex, and Aedes.
1. First, look at the wings. Look at the anterior border of the wings. If you see spots at the anterior margin of the wings, the mosquito is an “Anopheles”
2. If no spots are seen on the wings, then look at the body of the insect. If there are white stripes on a dark body including the legs, the mosquito is an “Aedes”
3. If none of the above features are seen, the displayed mosquito is a “Culex”
**Identification features of Anopheles adult:**
*   Body divided into three parts—head, thorax, and abdomen with a pair of wings and three pairs of legs
*   Wings spotted
*   Palpi long in both the sexes
*   What is the public health importance of this insect? A female Anopheles mosquito transmits malaria and filaria.
**Identification features of Aedes adult:**
*   Unspotted wings
*   Dark colored body
*   White stripes on black colored body. Also known as “Tiger Mosquito” due to its appearance
*   Palpi shorter than proboscis in females
*   What is the public health importance of this insect? A female Aedes mosquito transmits the following diseases: Dengue, dengue hemorrhagic fever, Chikungunya fever, chikungunya hemorrhagic fever, Yellow fever (not present in India), Rift valley fever, Filariasis (not in India).
**Identification features of Culex adult:**
*   Body divided into three parts—head, thorax, and abdomen with a pair of wings and three pairs of legs
*   Unspotted wings
*   Palpi shorter than the proboscis in females
*   It is also known as “Nuisance Mosquito.”
*   What is the public health importance of this insect? A female Culex mosquito transmits Bancroftian filariasis, Japanese encephalitis, West Nile fever, Viral arthritis.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Aedes aegypti index
gems.content:
**What is “Aedes aegypti index”?**
Aedes aegypti index is the ratio which is expressed as percentage of houses in a limited well-defined area on the premises of which actual breeding of Ae. Aegypti is found and the total number of houses examined in that area.
This index should not be more than 1% in towns and seaports in endemic areas to ensure control over yellow fever.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Screening Tests Matrix (Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV)
gems.content:
**Table Evaluation of a Screening Test**
| New test results | Disease present as diagnosed by the GS | Disease absent as diagnosed by the GS | Total |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| New test positive | True positive (TP) = a | False positive (FP) = b | a + b (all positives) |
| New test negative | False negative (FN) = c | True negative (TN) = d | c + d (all negatives) |
| Total | a + c (all patients) | b + d (all free of disease) |
**SENSITIVITY**
Sensitivity is defined as the ability of the test to detect all those with disease in the screened population. This is expressed as the proportion of those with disease correctly identified by a positive screening test result.
Sensitivity = Number of true positives / Total with disease = TP / TP + FN = a / (a + c)
Multiply by 100 to get sensitivity in percentage.
**SPECIFICITY**
Specificity is defined as the ability of the test to identify correctly those free of disease in the screened population. This is expressed as the proportion of those without disease correctly identified by a negative screening test result.
Specificity = Number of true negatives / Total without disease = TN / TN + FP = d / (b + d)
Multiply by 100 to get specificity in percentage.
**POSITIVE PREDICTIVE VALUE**
The PPV describes the probability of having the disease given a positive screening test result in the screened population. This is expressed as the proportion of those with disease among all screening test positives.
PPV = Number of true positives / Total test positives = TP / TP + FP = a / (a + b)
Multiply by 100 to get PPV in percentage.
**NEGATIVE PREDICTIVE VALUE**
The NPV describes the probability of not having the disease given a negative screening test result in the screened population. This is expressed as the proportion of those without disease among all screening test negatives.
NPV = Number of true negatives / Total test negatives = TN / FN + TN = d / (c + d)
Multiply by 100 to get NPV in percentage.
**EFFECT OF DISEASE PREVALENCE ON SCREENING VALUES**
Sensitivity and specificity are independent of prevalence of disease, i.e., these values are fixed for the particular test (they describe how well the screening test performs against the GS). These values will remain the same for the same test in different populations.
PPV and NPV are dependent upon the prevalence of the disease in the population studied, i.e., population specific. These values will change for the same test in different populations.
PPV and NPV give information on how well a screening test will perform in a given population with a specific prevalence. A higher prevalence will increase the PPV and decrease the NPV (by the same logic, lower prevalence will increase the NPV and decrease PPV).
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Relative Risk and Odds Ratio
gems.content:
**RELATIVE RISK**
Relative risk (RR) is the ratio of incidence of the outcome among the exposed to the incidence among the non-exposed individuals.
RR = Incidence among exposed / Incidence among nonexposed
RR is calculated from the data of a cohort study. This is because the incidence can be known only in a prospective study.
Incidence among exposed = a / a + b
Incidence among nonexposed = c / c + d
Hence, RR = a/(a + b) / c/(c + d)
**ODDS RATIO**
Odds ratio (OR) is the ratio of the odds (chance) of an event occurring in one group to the odds of it occurring in another group.
OR is calculated from the data of case control studies.
OR is calculated by the formula
OR = a × d / b × c = ad / bc
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Sampling Methods
gems.content:
**Probability Sampling Techniques**
*   **Simple Random Sampling**
*   **Systematic Sampling:** Sampling interval (k) is calculated: k = Total population / Sample size. Randomly select a random number between 1 and k. Continue to select every kth element.
*   **Stratified Sampling:** Divide the population into homogeneous groups or strata. Draw a simple random or systematic sample from each group.
*   **Multistage Sampling:** The sampling is employed for large area surveys, e.g., a state-level survey. (e.g., State -> Districts -> Taluks -> Villages).
*   **Cluster Sampling:** When the study population is naturally divided into clusters, such as hamlets, slums, factories, etc.
**Nonprobability Sampling Techniques**
*   **Convenience sampling:** Participants are selected at the convenience of the investigator.
*   **Purposive sampling:** Sampling units are selected if they have the characteristics that the investigator judges to be of value for the research.
*   **Quota sampling:** A fixed number of participants are to be included in each group of the population.
*   **Snowball sampling:** It is useful when it is challenging to find members of a particular population. Data are first collected from contacts known to the investigator. After that, the investigator requests from the responder for more possible respondents.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Bio-Medical Waste Management (BMWM) 2016 Color Coding
gems.content:
**Enumerate the categories of biomedical waste under the BMWM rules, 2016.**
*   **Yellow—NON chlorinated plastic bag**
    – Human anatomical waste
    – Animal anatomical waste
    – Soiled waste
    – Expired medicine
    – Chemical waste
    – Discarded linen or bedding
    – Microbiological waste
    *How is this bag disposed?* Incineration or plasma pyrolysis. Deep burial (ONLY in rural or remote areas where there is no access to CBMWTF).
*   **Red colored—NON chlorinated plastic bag**
    – Contaminated plastic waste (RECYLCLABLE)
    *How is this bag disposed?* Autoclaving/microwaving/hydroclaving followed by: Shredding or mutilation. Treated waste to be sent to registered recyclers for recycling.
*   **White (translucent) puncture proof containers**
    – Waste sharps including metals
    *How are the contents disposed?* Autoclaving/dry heat sterilization followed by: Shredding or mutilation. Treated waste to be sent to registered iron foundries for recycling or sanitary landfill or designated concrete waste sharp pit.
*   **Blue puncture proof containers (Blue cardboard box)**
    – Glass
    – Metallic implants
    *How are the contents disposed?* Disinfection or autoclaving/microwaving/hydroclaving. Treated waste is sent for recycling.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Chlorination of Water
gems.content:
**HORROCK’S APPARATUS**
**For what purpose will you use this?**
This is used to calculate the dose of bleaching powder required for disinfection of the given water sample.
**Appearance of blue color indicates what?**
It indicates the presence of free residual chlorine.
**What inference will you draw if no cup gave a blue color?**
Chlorine demand is not satisfied even in the sixth cup. This means that chlorine demand of the water sample is very high and probably the sampled water is polluted.
**What inference will you draw if all the cups gave a blue color?**
Chlorine demand has been satisfied in the first cup itself. Since the chlorine demand of the water sample is low only one scoopful (2 g) of bleaching powder is required to provide residual chlorine for 455 L of the sampled water.
**What is the minimum contact period of chlorination that you will allow before water is drawn for use from a well just chlorinated?**
A minimum contact period of 1 hour is allowed before the water is drawn for the purpose of drinking.
**What is the recommended level of residual chlorine in drinking water?**
The recommended level of “free” residual chlorine is 0.5 mg/L (ppm).
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Sewage Treatment
gems.content:
In modern sewage treatment plants, the principle of sewage purification is “biological.” This means that the sewage is purified by bacterial action initially by the action of anaerobic bacteria (anaerobic digestion of sewage), followed by the action of aerobic bacteria (aerobic digestion).
In these plants, sewage is treated in two stages:
1. **Primary treatment:** The solids are separated from the sewage and then subjected to anaerobic digestion. The solids are separated first by screening and then by sedimentation. *(Includes Screening, Grit Chamber, Primary Sedimentation Tank).*
2. **Secondary treatment:** The effluent is subjected to aerobic oxidation. *(Includes Trickling Filter Method or Activated Sludge Process, followed by Secondary Sedimentation Tank).*
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Modified Kuppuswamy Scale
gems.content:
This is the most commonly used scale for determining the SES of an urban family.
**Table Modified Kuppuswamy Scale for Urban Area (1976)**
| Component | Score categories |
| :--- | :--- |
| Education of head of family | Professional degree (7); Graduate (6); Intermediate/diploma (5); High school (4); Middle school (3); Primary school (2); Illiterate (1) |
| Occupation of head of family | Professional (10); Semi-profession (6); Clerical/shop/farm (5); Skilled worker (4); Semiskilled worker (3); Unskilled worker (2); Unemployed (1) |
| Total family income per month (as given originally in 1976) | `2000 and above (12); `1000-1999 (10); `750-999 (6); `500-749 (4); `300-499 (3); `101-299 (2); Less than `100 (1) |
**Table Socioeconomic Class Corresponding to the Total Score in Kuppuswamy's SES**
| Total score | Socioeconomic class |
| :--- | :--- |
| 26-29 | Upper class |
| 16-25 | Upper middle |
| 11-15 | Lower middle |
| 5-10 | Upper lower |
| Below 5 | Lower |
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: BG Prasad Scale
gems.content:
This is another scale for calculating the social class and is based on the monthly per capita income of the family. It has five classes as shown in the original 1961 scale.

The scale requires regular updating to account for inflation. The updated income limits are calculated using the latest Consumer Price Index for Industrial Workers (CPI-IW), which is released regularly on the official website of the Labour Bureau, Government of India (http://www.labourbureau.gov.in).

The formula to calculate the updated cut-offs using the current CPI-IW (Base year 2016 = 100) incorporates the linking factors for previous base years (1960, 1982, 2001, and 2016):
**New Income Limit = Original Value × (Current CPI-IW) × 2.88 × 4.63 × 4.93 / 100**

**Table: Original BG Prasad Scale (1961)**

| Monthly per capita income (₹) | Socioeconomic class |
| :--- | :--- |
| 100 and above | Upper class (I) |
| 50–99 | Upper middle class (II) |
| 30–49 | Middle class (III) |
| 15–29 | Lower middle class (IV) |
| Below 15 | Lower class (V) |

**Table: Recalculated Ranges of Per Capita Income Per Month for Prasad’s Scale (Updated for January 2026, CPI-IW = 148.6)**

| Monthly per capita income (₹) | Socioeconomic class |
| :--- | :--- |
| 9,769 and above | Upper class (I) |
| 4,885–9,768 | Upper middle class (II) |
| 2,931–4,884 | Middle class (III) |
| 1,465–2,930 | Lower middle class (IV) |
| Below 1,465 | Lower class (V) |

gems.title: Assessment of Overcrowding
gems.content:
Three criteria have been used commonly for judging the presence of “overcrowding.”
**Persons per Room Criterion**
*   One room: Two persons
*   Two rooms: Three persons
*   Three rooms: Five persons
*   Four rooms: Seven persons
*   Five rooms: 10 persons
*   More than five rooms: Add two persons to two for each additional room after five rooms
*   Note: An infant (a child under 12 months of age) is not counted. Children aged 1–10 years are counted as half a person each. All those above the age of 10 years are counted as one person each.
**Floor Space Criterion**
*   110 sq feet (11 sq m): Two persons
*   90–100 sq feet (9–10 sq m): One and half persons
*   70–90 sq feet (7–9 sq m): One person
*   50–70 sq feet (5–7 sq m): Half person
*   Less than 50 sq feet (5 sq m): Nil
*   The minimum standard for floor area per person has been set as 50 sq feet (5 sq. m).
**Sex Separation Criterion**
According to this criterion, overcrowding is considered to be present if two persons, above 9 years of age, of opposite sexes, not husband and wife, must sleep in the same room.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Checking Adequacy of Lighting
gems.content:
There are no published guidelines on the adequacy of lighting and how to check for light adequacy without instruments. However, the Illuminating Engineer Society (IES) has recommended that for casual reading an illumination level of 100 lux is sufficient. Also the Government of India recommends up to 100 lux of illumination in living rooms. Hence, it seems practical that if one is able to read news print in all the corners and the center of the room and also in the darkest portion of the room, the lighting can be considered adequate, provided there is no glare and the light does not directly fall in the eyes.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Nutrition Calculation & Terminology
gems.content:
**Concept of the “Consumption Unit”**
The energy consumption of an average male doing sedentary work is taken as one unit. The calorie requirement of women, children, and men doing moderate and heavy work is expressed as relative to that of a sedentary man. One unit of coefficient corresponds to the energy allowance for a sedentary man, which is 2110 kcal/day as per the ICMR, 2020 recommendations. This unit is the “Consumption Unit” (CU).
**Recommended Daily Allowance (RDA)**
The RDA is defined as the average daily dietary nutrient intake level sufficient to meet the nutrient requirement of nearly all (97–98%) healthy individuals in a particular life stage and gender group.
**Estimated Average Requirements (EAR)**
It is the average daily intake level that meets the requirement of half of the healthy population of the particular age, sex group, and stage of life. The ICMR recommends the use of EARs for assessing and planning diets.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: Family Definitions
gems.content:
*   **Family:** It is a group of biologically related individuals living together and eating from a common kitchen.
*   **Nuclear (elementary) family:** It consists of a married couple and their dependent children. A “new family” is a nuclear family within first 10 years of formation (marriage).
*   **Joint (extended) family:** It consists of a number of married couples and their children living in the same household. All the men in the household are related by blood and the women are their wives, unmarried daughters, or widows.
*   **Three-generation family:** It occurs when the married children of a couple continue to stay with their parents and have their own children. Hence, three generations related by direct descent live together.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: WHO Haemoglobin Cutoffs to Define Anaemia in Individuals and Populations
gems.content:
**WHO Haemoglobin Cutoffs to Define Anaemia in Individuals and Populations**
| Population | Haemoglobin concentration (g/dL) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Children, 6–23 months | < 10.5 |
| Children, 24–59 months | < 11.0 |
| Children, 5–11 years | < 11.5 |
| Children, 12–14 years, nonpregnant girls | < 12.0 |
| Children, 12–14 years, boys | < 12.0 |
| Adults, 15–65 years, nonpregnant women | < 12.0 |
| Adults, 15–65 years, men | < 13.0 |
| Pregnancy - First trimester | < 11.0 |
| Pregnancy - Second trimester | < 10.5 |
| Pregnancy - Third trimester | < 11.0 |
Note: The cut-offs and classifications for pregnancy are usually considered as the ones for the first or third trimester on average.
[REF]Reference: World Health Organization. Guideline on haemoglobin cutoffs to define anaemia in individuals and populations. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2024.[/REF]

gems.title: WHO Haemoglobin Cutoffs to Define Anaemia Severity in Individuals
gems.content:
**WHO Haemoglobin Cutoffs to Define Anaemia Severity in Individuals**
| Population | No anaemia (g/dL) | Mild anaemia (g/dL) | Moderate anaemia (g/dL) | Severe anaemia (g/dL) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Children, 6–23 months | ≥ 10.5 | 9.5–10.4 | 7.0–9.4 | < 7.0 |
| Children, 24–59 months | ≥ 11.0 | 10.0–10.9 | 7.0–9.9 | < 7.0 |
| Children, 5–11 years | ≥ 11.5 | 11.0–11.4 | 8.0–10.9 | < 8.0 |
| Children, 12–14 years, nonpregnant girls | ≥ 12.0 | 11.0–11.9 | 8.0–10.9 | < 8.0 |
| Children, 12–14 years, boys | ≥ 12.0 | 11.0–11.9 | 8.0–10.9 | < 8.0 |
| Adults, 15–65 years, nonpregnant women | ≥ 12.0 | 11.0–11.9 | 8.0–10.9 | < 8.0 |
| Adults, 15–65 years, men | ≥ 13.0 | 11.0–12.9 | 8.0–10.9 | < 8.0 |
| Pregnancy - First trimester | ≥ 11.0 | 10.0–10.9 | 7.0–9.9 | < 7.0 |
| Pregnancy - Second trimester | ≥ 10.5 | 9.5–10.4 | 7.0–9.4 | < 7.0 |
| Pregnancy - Third trimester | ≥ 11.0 | 10.0–10.9 | 7.0–9.9 | < 7.0 |
Note: The cut-offs and classifications for pregnancy are usually considered as the ones for the first or third trimester on average.
[REF]Reference: World Health Organization. Guideline on haemoglobin cutoffs to define anaemia in individuals and populations. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2024.[/REF]

gems.title: National Tuberculosis Elimination Program (NTEP)
gems.content:
**List the treatment categories under the National Tuberculosis Elimination Program (NTEP).**
There are two categories based on the evidence of drug resistance:
1. Drug-Sensitive TB:
    – INH (H)- and Rifampicin (R)-sensitive TB cases
    – Cases with unknown sensitivity pattern
2. Drug-Resistant TB (DR-TB): Evidence of resistance to one or more anti-TB drugs.
**What is the regimen for drug-sensitive TB (DSTB) patients?**
The regimen consists of two phases depicted by 2HRZE/4HRE:
1. Intensive phase:
    *   Isoniazid (H), rifampicin (R), pyrazinamide (Z), and ethambutol (E) in fixed drug combination (FDC) tablets
    *   Daily dose for 8 weeks (56 doses)
2. Continuation phase:
    *   Isoniazid (H), rifampicin (R), and ethambutol (E) in FDC tablets
    *   Daily dose for 16 weeks (112 doses)
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: National Leprosy Eradication Program (NLEP)
gems.content:
**Write the multidrug therapy for both types of leprosy.**
*   For MB leprosy
    – Rifampicin—600 mg once monthly in front of the health personnel
    – Clofazimine—300 mg once monthly and supervised along with rifampicin and 50 mg daily, self-administered along with Dapsone
    – Dapsone—100 mg daily, self-administered
*   For PB leprosy
    – Rifampicin—600 mg once a month for 6 months under supervision
    – Dapsone—100 mg daily for 6 months, self-administered
The duration of treatment for MB leprosy is 12 months and that of PB leprosy is 6 months.
[REF]Reference: Tiwari P, Tiwari S. Mastering Practicals: Community Medicine. 3rd ed. New Delhi: Wolters Kluwer (India) Pvt. Ltd.; 2023.[/REF]

gems.title: SRS Report 2024 (2022-24) Key Points
gems.content:
**Vital Indicators from Sample Registration System (SRS):**

| Vital Indicator | National Average | Rural Sector | Urban Sector | High-Burden / Highest Outlier | Best Performing / Lowest Outlier |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Total Fertility Rate (TFR) | 1.9 | 2.1 | 1.5 | Bihar (2.9) | Delhi (1.2), Kerala (1.3) |
| Crude Birth Rate (CBR) (per 1,000) | 18.3 | 20.2 | 14.7 | Bihar (26.8) | Kerala (11.1) |
| Crude Death Rate (CDR) (per 1,000) | 6.4 | 6.8 | 5.6 | Chhattisgarh (8.4) | Delhi (4.5) |

[REF]Reference: Sample Registration System (SRS) Report 2024.[/REF]

gems.title: NFHS-6 (2023-24) Key Points
gems.content:
**Key Indicators from National Family Health Survey (NFHS-6):**

| Category / Public Health Indicator | NFHS-6 Urban | NFHS-6 Rural | NFHS-6 National Total | NFHS-5 National Total | Key Epidemiological Trend |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Demography & Socio-Economics** ||||||
| Population under age 15 years | 22.0% | 27.0% | 25.5% | 26.5% | Shrinking young base; demographic transition. |
| Population age 60 years and above | 12.7% | 13.0% | 12.9% | 11.8% | Clear indication of programmatic aging. |
| Households with health insurance scheme | 56.4% | 62.0% | 60.2% | 41.0% | Massive rise due to PM-JAY & State expansions. |
| Usual female members owning a house/land | 18.2% | 19.1% | 18.8% | 14.0% | Modest improvement in physical asset ownership. |
| **Education & Digital Literacy** ||||||
| Women with 10 or more years of schooling | 61.5% | 39.7% | 46.4% | 41.0% | Steady growth in female secondary education. |
| Women who have ever used the internet | 77.3% | 58.6% | 64.3% | 33.3% | Exponential surge; narrowing digital divide. |
| Men who have ever used the internet | 87.1% | 77.1% | 80.5% | 51.2% | Continued expansion of digital footprint. |
| **Marriage & Fertility (TFR)** ||||||
| Women (20–24) married before age 18 | 11.4% | 23.3% | 20.1% | 23.3% | Notable reduction in child marriage pockets. |
| Total Fertility Rate (TFR) (children/woman) | 1.6 | 2.1 | 2.0 | 2.0 | Stabilized below the historical replacement level. |
| Teenage Pregnancy (Women age 15–19) | 3.4% | 7.9% | 6.7% | 6.8% | Stagnant; rural areas remain heavily high-burden. |
| **Contraception & Family Planning** ||||||
| Contraceptive Prevalence Rate (Any method) | 69.6% | 68.9% | 69.1% | 66.7% | Improved overall adoption of family planning. |
| Any Modern Contraceptive Method | 52.9% | 52.7% | 52.7% | 56.4% | Worrying drop in modern method adherence. |
| Any Traditional Contraceptive Method | 16.7% | 16.2% | 16.4% | 10.3% | Surge in traditional method use (spacing gap). |
| Female Sterilization (share of user base) | 32.6% | 38.1% | 36.5% | 37.9% | Remains heavily skewed towards terminal female load. |
| Male Sterilization (share of user base) | 0.4% | 0.5% | 0.5% | 0.3% | Persistent stagnation; high socio-cultural barrier. |
| Total Unmet Need for Family Planning | 7.0% | 9.1% | 8.5% | 9.4% | Marginal contraction in systemic supply gap. |
| **Maternal Care & Delivery Indicators** ||||||
| Mothers with 4+ Antenatal Care (ANC) visits | 75.8% | 61.4% | 65.2% | 58.5% | Robust expansion of quality ANC tracking. |
| Mothers consuming IFA for 100+ days | 67.4% | 50.3% | 54.9% | 44.1% | Positive growth for Anaemia Mukt Bharat. |
| Mothers consuming IFA for 180+ days | 49.2% | 33.7% | 37.8% | 26.0% | Commendable increase in programmatic compliance. |
| Institutional Births | 95.2% | 89.0% | 90.6% | 88.6% | India crosses the 90% institutional delivery mark. |
| Total Caesarean Section (C-Section) Rate | 40.5% | 22.8% | 27.2% | 21.5% | Critical rise; far exceeding WHO's 15% line. |
| C-Section Rate in Private Facilities | 57.4% | 52.2% | 54.1% | 47.4% | Over half of private births are surgical. |
| C-Section Rate in Public Facilities | 28.0% | 14.0% | 16.9% | 14.3% | Gradual shift upward in public delivery pathways. |
| **Child Vaccinations (12–23 months)** ||||||
| Full Immunization (Card + Recall data) | 81.0% | 83.1% | 82.6% | 76.6% | Significant gain; rural blocks outperform urban. |
| 3 doses of Rotavirus Vaccine | 84.0% | 85.9% | 85.4% | 36.4% | Massive universal scale-up success under UIP. |
| Birth dose of Hepatitis B Vaccine | 80.0% | 76.8% | 77.6% | 67.4% | Substantial boost in early neonatal safety grids. |
| **Child Nutrition (Under 5 Years)** ||||||
| Stunting (Height-for-Age chronic matrix) | 23.9% | 30.9% | 29.3% | 35.5% | Major programmatic success; falls below 30%. |
| Wasting (Weight-for-Height acute matrix) | 16.5% | 19.8% | 19.0% | 19.3% | Rigid baseline; slow response to interventions. |
| Severe Wasting (SAM marker) | 4.6% | 5.4% | 5.2% | 7.7% | Strong reduction in critical malnutrition morbidity. |
| Underweight (Weight-for-Age composite) | 25.3% | 33.8% | 31.8% | 32.1% | Plateued; minimal net reduction. |
| Exclusive Breastfeeding (under 6 months) | 54.5% | 56.2% | 55.8% | 63.7% | Severe setback; significant dip in IYCF metrics. |
| **Adult Metabolic & NCD Risk Profiles** ||||||
| Overweight/Obese Women (BMI ≥ 25) | 42.8% | 25.5% | 30.7% | 24.0% | Accelerating NCD epidemiological risk among females. |
| Overweight/Obese Men (BMI ≥ 25) | 36.3% | 23.0% | 27.3% | 22.9% | Clear metabolic shift toward urbanized lifestyles. |
| High Blood Sugar or on Medication (Women) | 21.9% | 16.2% | 17.8% | 13.5% | Severe expansion of diabetes screening burden. |
| High Blood Sugar or on Medication (Men) | 23.9% | 19.7% | 20.9% | 15.6% | One-fifth of adult male cohort exhibits high risk. |
| Elevated Blood Pressure or on Meds (Women) | 23.5% | 17.8% | 19.4% | 21.3% | Slight downward correction in hypertensive load. |
| Elevated Blood Pressure or on Meds (Men) | 26.2% | 20.4% | 22.1% | 24.0% | Moderate reduction; remains high in urban centers. |
| **Social Determinants & Safety** ||||||
| Menstrual Hygienic Protection (15–24 years) | 90.7% | 75.0% | 79.2% | 77.6% | Continued improvement in adolescent female health. |
| Ever Experienced Spousal Violence | 17.5% | 24.4% | 22.3% | 29.2% | Promising decline in gender-based physical burden. |

[REF]Reference: National Family Health Survey (NFHS-6), Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India.[/REF]

gems.title: Analysis of New Parameters Added in NFHS-6
gems.content:
NFHS-6 radically widened its scope to catch up with structural transformations in governance and evolving clinical priorities:

**1. High-Yield Infectious Disease Seroprevalence (CAB Testing Expansion)**
*   **The Parameter:** The Clinical, Anthropometric, and Biochemical (CAB) testing protocols have been expanded to include diagnostic biomarker screening for HIV, Hepatitis-B, and Hepatitis-C among adult men and women.
*   **Child Intervention:** It also introduced Dried Blood Spot (DBS) testing for Hepatitis-B among children aged 4–5 years.
*   **MD Public Health Impact:** Historically, India relied on programmatic surveillance data (such as NACO or localized sentinel surveillance). NFHS-6 offers the first integrated, large-scale community seroprevalence database for these three blood-borne viral pathogens, enabling precise epidemiological mapping of hidden hotspots.

**2. Social Welfare and Governance Metrics**
*   **The Parameter:** New questions tracking Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) deployment and Self-Help Group (SHG) institutional penetration.
*   **MD Public Health Impact:** Evaluates the functional interaction between women’s financial autonomy and health-seeking behaviors. It shifts the analytical perspective of community health away from purely clinical metrics, contextualizing it within social safety nets.

**3. Advanced Digital & Financial Behavior Integration**
*   **The Parameter:** Comprehensive parameters detailing individual digital literacy, mobile phone utilization patterns, and autonomous financial transactions.
*   **MD Public Health Impact:** Directly measures the target readiness for Digital Health interventions (like Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission/ABDM and tele-consultation models).

CRITICAL DELETIONS AND THE OMISSION OF ANAEMIA
One of the most heavily debated changes in NFHS-6 was the structural removal of key parameters:
*   **The Omission of Anaemia:** Biomarker mapping for Anaemia (which stood at a staggering 67.1% for children and 57.2% for women in NFHS-5) has been entirely dropped from NFHS-6.
*   **The Institutional Justification:** The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare argued that previous rounds used capillary blood finger-pricks, which are prone to dilutions and altitude/smoking over-adjustments, thereby producing inflated or flawed statistical trends. Anaemia has now been shifted to the dedicated Diet and Biomarkers Survey in India (DABS-1) conducted by ICMR-NIN using more precise venous blood samples.
*   **Other Critical Exclusions:** Questions tracking Disability prevalence, along with anthropometric parameters like waist-hip ratio and waist circumference, were also entirely omitted from this round.

HIGH-YIELD ANALYTICAL CRITIQUES FOR UNIVERSITY BOARD EXAMS

**1. The Paradoxical Collapse of Spacing Contraception**
The TFR has successfully dropped to 2.0, but the underlying mix of family planning methods has destabilized. Modern contraceptive methods dropped from 56.4% to 52.7%, while unreliable, traditional spacing methods spiked from 10.3% to 16.4%.
*   **Analysis:** This indicates an acute behavioral fatigue or systemic supply-chain failure in temporary spacing logistics (e.g., condoms, oral pills, injectables). Couples are shifting away from institutional modern options while desperately attempting to manage fertility via traditional methods.

**2. The Private Obstetric C-Section Crisis**
The total C-section rate has jumped to 27.2%, driven primarily by the private sector at an astonishing 54.1%.
*   **Analysis:** This highlights commercialized medicalization. Over half of deliveries in private configurations bypass natural pathways. In university responses, this calls for the immediate structural mandates of regular clinical audits, standardizing birthing fees, and shifting the workforce focus toward midwifery-led models within public centers.

**3. The Nutritional Decoupling: Stunting Success vs. Breastfeeding Failure**
Stunting has shown a brilliant downward shift, settling at 29.3%. However, exclusive breastfeeding plummeted from 63.7% to 55.8%.
*   **Analysis:** This exposes a clear public health mismatch. The drop in stunting reflects structural improvements under POSHAN 2.0 (e.g., intensive complementary feeding, maternal literacy, clean water). However, the sudden fall in exclusive breastfeeding reflects a breakdown in primary healthcare counseling (IYCF protocols) at the sub-center and ASHA delivery levels, likely impacted by the rapid commercial marketing of infant milk substitutes.

[REF]Reference: National Family Health Survey (NFHS-6), Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India.[/REF]
